coredata.dc:title: Dual-signal uric acid sensing based on carbon quantum dots and o-phenylenediamine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Chunling
Qin, Xiu
Xu, Yuanjin
Shi, Rui
Cheng, Shiqi
Wang, Yilin
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which showed excitation-dependent emission characteristics, were prepared using a facile hydrothermal method. The structure and optical properties of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. These CQDs also showed peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with an absorption peak at 420 nm. DAP exhibited an obvious fluorescence emission at 550 nm under the excitation of 360 nm. On the other hand, it decreased the fluorescence of CQDs at 450 nm via inner filter effect. The experimental results indicated that the H2O2 concentration affected the color of DAP and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and DAP. Thus, a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-signal method was established for measuring the concentrations of H2O2 and uric acid (UA). The effects of pH, incubation temperature, incubation time, and OPD concentration on the response were investigated. Under the conditions of pH 7.5, temperature 50 °C, incubation time 30 min, and OPD 1.5 mM, the absorbance and fluorescence intensity ratio responses were linearly dependent on UA concentration ranging from 5.0 μM to 100 μM. The limits of detection were 0.7 and 0.5 μM with a colorimetric method and ratiometric fluorescence method, respectively. More importantly, this dual responsive method has been applied to the determination of UA in urine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Colorimetric
Fluorescence
Detection
Uric acid
Carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of peroxidase-like activity of CQDs
Procedures for H2O2 and UA detection
Microstructure and spectral characterization of CQDs
Feasibility and response mechanism of the method
Determination of UA in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CQDs, (B) particle size distribution histogram.
(A) FTIR spectrum of CQDs; (B) C 1s, (C) N 1s, and (D) O 1s of XPS spectra of CQDs.
(A) Absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra of the CQDs. (the insets show the pictures of CQDs in daylight (left) and UV light (right). (B) Excitation-dependent emission spectra of the CQDs.
Absorbance spectra of (a) OPD + H2O2 + CQDs, (b) OPD + H2O2 + CQDs + isopropanol, and (c) OPD + H2O2 + CQDs + methanol in NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 buffer at pH 7.5. Inset: the corresponding photographs.
(A) Absorbance spectrum of DAP and emission spectrum of CQDs. (B) The fluorescence lifetime spectra of CQDs and CQDs + DAP.

(A) Absorbance spectra of CQDs-OPD with different concentrations of UA. Inset: images of the corresponding solution. (B) Linear fitting curve of absorbance at 420 nm vs. UA concentration. (C) emission spectra of CQDs - OPD with different concentrations of UA. Inset: fluorescence imaging of corresponding solution. (D) Linear fitting curve of I 550/I 450 vs. UA concentration.
(A) Selectivity of colorimetric (A) and ratiometric fluorescence (B) for UA detection. The concentration of UA was 0.1 mM, whereas that of other substances was 1.0 mM.
Schematic illustration of double signal determination of H2O2 and UA.
Comparison of the analytical methodologies based on CQDs or CQDs composites for detecting UA.
Detection of UA in human urine.

head.ce:title: Dual-signal uric acid sensing based on carbon quantum dots and o-phenylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Nanning 530004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Guangxi University, Guangxi Key Laboratory of Electrochemical Energy Materials, Nanning 530004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which showed excitation-dependent emission characteristics, were prepared using a facile hydrothermal method. The structure and optical properties of CQDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence spectroscopy. These CQDs also showed peroxidase-like activity and could catalyze the H2O2-mediated oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to form 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with an absorption peak at 420 nm. DAP exhibited an obvious fluorescence emission at 550 nm under the excitation of 360 nm. On the other hand, it decreased the fluorescence of CQDs at 450 nm via inner filter effect. The experimental results indicated that the H2O2 concentration affected the color of DAP and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and DAP. Thus, a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-signal method was established for measuring the concentrations of H2O2 and uric acid (UA). The effects of pH, incubation temperature, incubation time, and OPD concentration on the response were investigated. Under the conditions of pH 7.5, temperature 50 °C, incubation time 30 min, and OPD 1.5 mM, the absorbance and fluorescence intensity ratio responses were linearly dependent on UA concentration ranging from 5.0 μM to 100 μM. The limits of detection were 0.7 and 0.5 μM with a colorimetric method and ratiometric fluorescence method, respectively. More importantly, this dual responsive method has been applied to the determination of UA in urine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA), an important biomolecule in body fluid, is the final product of purine metabolism [1]. The concentration of UA ranges from 0.12 mM to 0.46 mM in the serum of healthy people and from 1.4 mM to 4.5 mM in urine [2]. The content of UA can reflect the immune function and metabolism of the human body [3]. Abnormally high levels of UA in fluid are closely associated with some diseases, such as gout, hyperuricemia, and Lesch–Nyhan syndrome, whereas extremely low levels of UA may lead to multiple sclerosis or oxidative stress [4,5]. Hence, the accurate determination of UA in body fluid is important for disease diagnosis. At present, a variety of analytical techniques have been developed for the determination of UA, including liquid chromatography [6,7], electrochemistry [8,9], and optical techniques [10,11]. Each of these techniques has its own advantages and disadvantages. Optical techniques have attracted wide attention due to its high sensitivity, fast response, low cost, and facile operation [12]. Colorimetry and fluorescence are the most common optical analysis methods [13]. Various nanomaterials, such as CoP nanosheets [14], gold/silver nanocluster [15], and MoS2 nanoflakes [16], have been used for colorimetric or fluorescence determination of UA. The general fluorescence analysis is based on single fluorescence measurement, and its results are easily affected by excitation source, background signal, and environmental conditions [17]. Ratiometric fluorescence analysis is based on dual-fluorescence-signal measurement at the same excitation wavelength, which has been widely used in chemical and biological sensing [18,19]. The ratio of fluorescent intensity at two emission wavelengths is capable of built-in correction to effectively minimize the impact of the environment and greatly improve the sensitivity and accuracy [20]. Most ratiometric fluorescent probes are organic fluorescent materials [21,22]. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent probes based on inorganic fluorescent nanomaterials have also been developed [17].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel nanomaterials that have been widely used as fluorescent probes [23,24], fluorescent imaging indicators [25,26], photo-catalysts [27,28], and peroxidase mimetics [29,30]. Compared with other fluorescent materials and artificial enzymes, CQDs are superior in terms of stability, cost, water solubility, and biocompatibility [31]. Because of its simple operation and good safety, hydrothermal method is a common method for the preparation of CQDs [32]. The raw materials for preparing CQDs by hydrothermal method can be chemical reagents [22] or biomass [32–34]. The fluorescence [35–39], colorimetric [40] and electrochemical [41,42] methods based on CQDs or CQDs composites have been used for UA detection (Table 1 ). The research showed that UA could quench the fluorescence of CQDs prepared from pork, and the quenching degree was related to the concentration of UA [35]. In the presence of H2O2, Ag@CQDs nanocomposites can catalyze the oxidation of 3, 3′, 5, 5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form blue oxidized TMB, which can be reduced by UA to form colorless TMB. Thus, the determination of UA can be achieved through Ag@CQDs-catalyzed color reaction [40]. Compared with Fe3O4 nanoparticles modified electrode, the sensitivity of Fe3O4@CQDs nanocomposites modified electrode for the electrochemical determination of UA was greatly improved [41]. However, most of the CQDs used in the above research were prepared from chemical reagents, and all mentioned above are single signal detection. ZnFe2O4 magnetic microspheres can catalyze the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) by H2O2 to generate 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP) with yellow color and fluorescence emission at 556 nm. The presence of DAP results in the decrease of fluorescence at 430 nm of B, N co-doped CQDs. On the basis of these phenomena, Li’s group established a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent double signals sensor platform for the detection of H2O2 and H2O2-related analytes [43].

In the present study, fluorescent CQDs with peroxidase-like activity were prepared from biomass, which could catalyze the oxidation of OPD by H2O2 to generate yellow DAP with an obvious absorption peak at 420 nm and an emission peak at 550 nm (Scheme 1 ). Considering that the excitation spectrum of CQDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of DAP, the fluorescence of CQDs is quenched by DAP via the inner filter effect (IFE). Results demonstrated that the H2O2 concentration affected not only the color of DAP but also the fluorescence emission of DAP and CQDs. With the increase of H2O2 concentration, the absorbance of DAP at 420 nm and the fluorescence emission at 550 nm increased, whereas the fluorescence emission of CQDs at 450 nm decreased. Thus, the H2O2 concentration can be determined by measuring the absorbance at 420 nm and fluorescence intensity ratio (I 550/I 450). As H2O2 is one of the products of UA catalytic oxidation, a simple and sensitive dual-signaling (colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence) method for UA detection was established. The proposed method has the following advantages: (1) CQDs are not only used as a peroxidase mimic but also as a fluorescent material, which are easy to prepare and have good biocompatibility and high stability. (2) The chromogenic substrate (OPD) is colorless, while the chromogenic product (DAP) is yellow. There is no background interference in colorimetric analysis. (3) Colorimetric and ratio fluorescence dual-signal analysis are more reliable and accurate than single signal detection. As far as we know, this colorimetric and ratio fluorescence dual-signal UA assay using CQDs as mimic enzyme and fluorescent probe has not been reported.

All the chemical and biological reagents were purchased from commercial suppliers. Doubly deionized water was utilized for the preparation of all aqueous solutions. UA, uricase and OPD were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Threonine (Thr), serine (Ser), histidine (His), cysteine (Cys), and glycine (Gly) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, H2O2, NaOH, KCl, MgCl2, and glucose (Glu) were supplied by Guanghua Sci-Tech Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China).

The morphology of CQDs was analyzed with a Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CQDs was obtained on a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CQDs was performed with an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission measurements were obtained with a Unico 4802 UV–Vis spectrophotometer and a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. Fluorescence lifetime decays were measured with a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

Fluorescent CQDs with peroxidase-like activity were synthesized in accordance with our previous publication with some modifications [44,45], and the procedure is provided in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of peroxidase-like activity of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase-like activity of CQDs was studied through the oxidation of OPD with H2O2. Typically, 200 μL of CQDs was introduced into a colorimetric cylinder containing 750 μL of OPD (10 mM), 500 μL of H2O2 (5 mM), and 3.55 mL of NaH2PO4- Na2HPO4 (10 mM, pH 7.5). The UV–Vis spectrum was obtained after reaction at room temperature for 15 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedures for H2O2 and UA detection
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 detection, 500 μL of H2O2 with various concentration, 750 μL of OPD (10 mM) and 200 μL of CQDs were added to NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 buffer (3.55 mL, 10 mM, pH 7.5). Then, the mixed solution was incubated at 50 °C for 30 min. The UV–Vis spectra were scanned in the range of 330–550 nm, and the fluorescence emission spectra (excited at 360 nm) were recorded.

For UA detection, 500 μL of UA (pH 8.0, adjusted with 10 mM NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4) with different concentration and 100 μL of 1.0 mg/mL uricase were incubated for 30 min at 37 °C to produce H2O2. Subsequently, 750 μL of OPD (10 mM), 200 μL of CQDs, and 3.45 mL of NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 (10 mM, pH 7.5) were added successively. The reaction solution was incubated at 50 °C for another 30 min. The corresponding UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were then recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microstructure and spectral characterization of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CQDs were characterized by TEM, FTIR, XPS, UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CQDs are nearly spherical nanoparticles with good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). A lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (insert of Fig. 1A) corresponding to (100) facet of graphite indicates that the CQDs contain graphite-like structure [46]. The statistics revealed that the size distribution of CQDs is in the range of 1.47 to 4.97 nm with an average particle diameter of 3.05 ± 0.39 nm based on measurements of 60 random particles (Fig. 1B).

FTIR spectroscopy was performed to analyze the groups within CQDs (Fig. 2 A). The FTIR spectrum shows the characteristic absorption bands of NH and OH bonds stretching vibrations at 3422 cm−1, CH bond stretching vibrations at 2948 cm−1, respectively. The peak at 1651 cm−1 is attributed to CO and CC groups. The absorptions at 1402 and 1113 cm−1 indicate the existence of COH and COC bonds within CQDs. XPS was employed to further identify the surface states and elements of the resultant CQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2B) showed three peaks at 284.5, 285.4, and 287.7 eV, which were ascribed to CC, CN, and CO bonds, respectively [47]. The spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 2C) displayed two peaks at 399.0 and 400.1 eV, which were assigned to CNC and NH groups [48]. The spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 2D) consisted of two peaks at 531.5 and 536.8 eV, which indicated the existence of CO and COC/COH groups [49]. In a word, the FTIR results were in good agreement with XPS data, which confirmed the existence of COOH, OH, and NH2 groups on the surface of CQDs, and ensured the good hydrophilicity of CQDs.

UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were performed to analyze the photophysical behavior of CQDs. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 A) displays two peaks at 275 nm corresponding to the π–π* transition of the aromatic CC, and 400 nm ascribed to the n–π* transition of CO band [50]. The as-prepared CQDs are yellowish in sunlight and emit blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light (insert of Fig. 3A). The excitation and fluorescence spectra of CQDs have a mirror symmetry, the maximum emission wavelength is 450 nm, and the corresponding optimal excitation wavelength is 360 nm. As shown in Fig. 3B, the CQDs exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission characteristics with the maximum emission wavelength ranging from 430 nm to 483 nm at excitation from 300 nm to 420 nm. This phenomenon may be related to the surface defect of CQDs [51]. The quantum yield (QY) of CQDs was calculated to be 13.6% with quinine sulfate (54%) as the reference, which was close to or higher than that of other biomass-originated CQDs [34,48,52]. CQDs with a good QY are due to the fluorescence enhancement of O- and N-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs.

To evaluate the peroxidase-like activity of the prepared CQDs, controlled experiments were performed. As observed in Fig. S1, the mixture of OPD and CQDs shows no absorption in the UV–Vis spectra (curve a). The OPD solution containing H2O2 presents a weak absorption peak at 420 nm (curve b), suggesting that H2O2 can slowly oxidize OPD to DAP. However, OPD involving H2O2 in the presence of CQDs exhibits a strong absorption peak at 420 nm (curve c) which verifies the catalytic performance of CQDs. The color change from colorless to bright yellow (insert of Fig. S1) further supports the above conclusion. These results indicate that the as-prepared CQDs exhibit peroxidase-like activity in the oxidation of OPD.

Hydroxyl radical (
OH) scavengers, such as methanol and isopropanol, are usually employed to explore whether the peroxidase-like activity of CQDs was related to the production of
OH radicals [51]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the presence of methanol and isopropanol lightens the color of DAP and decreases the absorbance of DAP at 420 nm, which can be attributed to the incomplete oxidation of OPD due to the consumption of
OH radicals by methanol and isopropanol. These results indicate that CQDs could catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to generate
OH and contributed to the oxidation of OPD to form a colored product (DAP).

To further evaluate the peroxidase-like activity, steady-state kinetic analysis of CQD-catalyzed OPD oxidation was performed. The absorbance of oxidation product DAP at 420 nm was converted to concentration by using the Beer–Lambert law with a molar absorption coefficient of 1.67 × 104 M−1 cm−1 for DAP [53]. The initial velocity of the reaction was calculated and plotted against OPD or H2O2 concentration. As shown in Fig. S2A and S2B, the CQD-catalyzed OPD oxidation conform to the Michaelis–Menten model. K m (Michaelis constant) and V max (maximum reaction velocity) are acquired from the Lineweaver-Burk equation:
(1)
1 V
=
K m
V max
·
1
S
+
1
V max
where [S] and V are the substrate concentration and initial reaction velocity, respectively. K m is usually used as an indicator of enzyme affinity to substrate, a small K m value indicates strong affinity between the substrate and the enzyme. According to the Lineweaver-Burk plots in Fig. S2C and S2D, the K m values of CQDs toward OPD and H2O2 were found to be 2.20 and 0.23 mM, respectively. The K m value of CQDs for OPD is higher than that of horseradish peroxidase (0.59 mM) [54], demonstrating that the affinity between CQDs and OPD is weaker than that between horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and OPD. However, the K m value of CQDs for H2O2 is lower than that of HRP (0.34 mM) [54], suggesting a low concentration of H2O2 is required for the CQDs to reach V max compared with the HRP. These results reveal that the obtained CQDs possessed good catalytic activity and could be used as efficient peroxidase mimics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and response mechanism of the method
ce:section.ce:para: In the presence of H2O2, OPD is oxidized to form yellow DAP with an absorption peak at 420 nm. With the increase of H2O2 concentration, the color of the solution gradually deepens, and the absorbance at 420 nm increases. Thus, the colorimetric response can be used for H2O2 detection. CQDs emits blue fluorescence with an emission peak at 450 nm (λ ex = 360 nm). The absorption spectrum of DAP largely overlaps with the emission spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 5 A). The presence of DAP decreases the fluorescence of CQDs (Scheme 1). DAP itself has an emission peak at 550 nm. The higher the concentration of H2O2 is, the more DAP is produced, which leads to the stronger fluorescence at 550 nm and the weaker fluorescence at 450 nm. Thus, the ratiometric fluorescence response (I 550/I 450) can also be used for H2O2 detection. Since H2O2 is one of the products of UA catalytic oxidation, this dual-signal analysis method can be further used to indirectly determine UA.

The mechanism for the decrease of CQD fluorescence was studied on the basis of the absorption and emission spectra. The spectral overlapping shown in Fig. 5A suggested that the IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) may occur between CQDs and DAP. To further confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, zeta potential and fluorescence lifetime were measured. The absorption spectra of DAP in the absence and presence of CQDs almost overlap (Fig. S3A), which indicated that no complex was formed and FRET between DAP and CQDs could not occur [10]. In NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 (10 mM, pH = 7.5) buffer medium, the zeta potentials of CQDs and DAP are −37.62 and −24.31 mV, respectively (Fig. S3B). They are all negatively charged, the distance between DAP and CQDs should be greater than 10 nm, indicating that the FRET could not occur. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs hardly changed before and after the addition of DAP, indicating that there was no energy or electron transfer between CQDs and DAP [38]. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that the mechanism of the quenching of CQDs fluorescence was attributed to IFE.

To obtain better sensitivity for the established method, several important parameters such as pH, temperature, incubation time, and OPD concentration were optimized. I 550/I 450 was used to evaluate the signal response effect, where I 550 and I 450 denote the emission intensity at 550 and 450 nm, respectively. The influence of pH ranging from 5.5 to 9.0 on the signal response was studied. As shown in Fig. S4A, the value of I 550/I 450 first increases and then decreases with increasing pH value. The maximum value of I 550/I 450 is obtained at pH 7.5, which was adopted for further experiments. The influences of incubation temperature ranging from 25 °C to 60 °C on I 550/I 450 were also examined, and the results are shown in Fig. S4B. In a water bath at 50 °C, the CQDs–H2O2–OPD system obtained the best response, and the value of I 550/I 450 reached the maximum. As presented in Fig. S4C, with the increase of incubation time, the value of I 550/I 450 also increased and basically reached a stable state at 30 min. To ensure maximum fluorescence ratio and obtain a stable signal, 30 min was chosen as the incubation time. The I 550/I 450 increased gradually with increasing OPD concentration and reached a balance when the concentration was 1.5 mM (Fig. S4D). Thus, the experiment was carried out with the following parameters: pH 7.5, temperature 50 °C, incubation time 30 min and OPD concentration 1.5 mM.

Under the optimal conditions, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CQDs-OPD with varying concentrations of H2O2 were recorded. As shown in Fig. S5A, the absorption intensity at 420 nm increased gradually with increasing concentration of H2O2, along with an obvious color change from colorless to yellow, which can be distinguished by naked eye (insert of Fig. S5A). The absorbance is linearly related to H2O2 in the 5.0 to 100 μΜ concentration range (Fig. S5B) with a regression equation A 420 = 0.003C H2O2 + 0.0454 (R 2 = 0.9992). Fig. S5C shows that the fluorescence emission of CQDs at 450 nm decreases gradually, and that of DAP at 550 nm increases with increasing H2O2 concentration. A good linear relationship was observed between the value of I 550/I 450 and the concentration of H2O2 ranging from 10.0 μΜ to 250 μΜ (Fig. S5D). The linear regression equation can be expressed as I 550/I 450 = 0.005C H2O2 + 0.2972 with a coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.9952. The limits of detection (LODs) based on 3σ/K (where σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank solution measurements, σ = 6.6 × 10−4 for colorimetry, σ = 1.0 × 10−3 for fluorescence method, and K is the slope of calibration curve) for H2O2 were 0.7 (colorimetry) and 0.6 μΜ (ratiometric fluorescence). The analytical performance of the proposed method was compared with that of reported methods. As can be seen from Table S1, our method is better than or comparable to most of the methods for H2O2 detection in sensitivity and linear range.

Considering that UA can be oxidized to generate H2O2 in the presence of uricase, a method for detecting UA was established. Fig. 6 A shows the absorbance spectra of DAP dependent on the concentration of UA. As can be seen, with the increase of UA concentration, the absorbance at 420 nm increases gradually, accompanied with obvious color change (insert of Fig. 6A). As shown in Fig. 6B, the absorbance at 420 nm is linearly correlated with the concentration of UA ranging from 5.0 μΜ to 100 μΜ with a regression equation: A 420 = 0.003C UA + 0.0388 (R 2 = 0.9912). As can be seen from Fig. 6C, with the increase of UA concentration, the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 550 nm increases, whereas that of CQDs at 450 nm decreases. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 550/I 450) is proportional to the UA concentration from 5.0 μΜ to 100 μΜ (Fig. 6D) with a regression equation of I 550/I 450 = 0.006C UA + 0.191 (R 2 = 0.9946). The LODs of colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescent methods were 0.7 and 0.5 μM, respectively, indicating that this method has a high sensitivity. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) for five parallel measurements of 60 μM UA by colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence were 1.0% and 3.2%, respectively, indicating good repeatability of the method. Compared with the previously reported methods, the proposed dual-signal UA detection method shows superior performance in terms of LOD and linear range (Table S2). Compared with the different analytical methodologies for detection of UA based on carbon nanomaterials, the CQDs of this work does not need to be specifically doped with other elements and specific modification, it can also achieve higher sensitivity and wider linear range. Hence, the developed method had the advantages of high sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity.

To evaluate the selectivity of this method in UA detection, the most common coexisting substances such as Na+, K+, Mg2+, Glu, and a series of amino acids (Cys, Gly, His, Ser, and Thr), which may interfere with UA detection in human urine, were investigated. The experiments were carried out according to the procedure described in Section 2.4, and the concentration of these interferences was 10 times higher than that of UA. As expected, only UA caused a significant increase in absorbance at 420 nm and fluorescence intensity ratio (I 550/I 450) (Fig. 7 A and B). The high selectivity is due to the specificity of uricase, which can only catalyze the oxidation of UA to produce H2O2. These results suggested that the proposed method displays high selectivity and reliability for UA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the feasibility of the proposed method, the concentration of UA in human urine was measured. Urine samples of healthy people were taken from two volunteers in our laboratory, and the supernatant was extracted after centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 5 min. Taking into consideration the level of UA in healthy human urine and the linear range of the proposed method, 0.1 mL of supernatant and different concentrations of UA were added to the reaction system and diluted to 5.0 mL, which was a 50-fold dilution. The results are shown in Table 2 . The results obtained by colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence assays are basically consistent. The recoveries of UA in urine ranged from 94.0% to 104.5%, and the RSD ranged from 1.7% to 6.8%. More importantly, the urine sample from a patient with hyperuricemia was tested using the proposed method and clinical method. The UA content detected by the proposed method was 6.8 mM, and that of the clinical method was 7.2 mM. The results demonstrated that the relative error of the two methods was 5.6%, indicating the reliability of the proposed method. The above results displayed the good potential of the method based on colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence method for the detection of UA in real samples.

In summary, a colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence assay for the detection of UA base on CQDs was developed. This dual-mode sensing system posses three obvious advantages: (1) the system does not require complex synthesis process and specific modification, making the operation more convenient; (2) the CQDs-UA-OPD response system shows high sensitivity and excellent selectivity; (3) the sensing platform can output colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence dual-readout signal, making the results more convincing. The detection limits of UA were 0.7 μΜ for colorimetric and 0.5 μΜ for ratiometric fluorescence, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed method has been applied for the determination of UA in human urine with satisfactory results, indicating its promising potential applications.

Chunling Yuan: Methodology, Conceptualization, Writing - original draft. Xiu Qin: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yuanjin Xu: Writing - review & editing. Rui Shi: Data curation, Investigation. Shiqi Cheng: Software. Yilin Wang: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding for this work was from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61664002) and the Innovation Project of Guangxi Graduate Education (No. YCSW2020016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119678.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A highly selective and sensitive colorimetric uric acid biosensor based on Cu(II)-catalyzed oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.-F. Lu, J.-Y. Li, M.-M. Zhang, D. Wu, Q.-L. Zhang, A highly selective and sensitive colorimetric uric acid biosensor based on Cu(II)-catalyzed oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 244 (2017) 77-83. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.12.127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoclusters as switch-off fluorescent probe for detection of uric acid based on the inner filter effect of hydrogen peroxide-mediated enlargement of gold nanoparticles
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, H. Li, B. Guo, L. Wei, B. Chen, Y. Zhang, Gold nanoclusters as switch-off fluorescent probe for detection of uric acid based on the inner filter effect of hydrogen peroxide-mediated enlargement of gold nanoparticles, Biosens. Bioelectron. 91 (2017) 734-740. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2017.01.020.

J. Electroanal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhao, J. Zhao, L. Qin, H. Jia, S. Liu, ynthesis of Ta/Ni microcavity array film for highly sensitive uric acid detection, J. Electroanal. Chem. (834) 2019 86-93. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.12.053.

J. Electroanal, Chem.
